Fern Polrrrcar- PRACrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
February 20,2006
Elizabeth Wagner Hull
Assistant City Attorney
City of Chula Vista
276 FourthAvenue
Chula Vista, CA 91910
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. ,4.-06-219
Dear Ms. Hull:
This letter responds to your requests for advicel on behalf of Chula Vista
Councilmembers Steve Castaneda, John McCann, Jerry Rindone, and Councilmember-
elect Rudy Ramlrez regarding the conflict of interest piovisions of the political Reform
Act (the *Act').z
QUESTION
May Councilmernbers Castaneda, McCann, Rindone, and Councilmember-elect
Ramirez pafiicipate in governmental decisions to modiff the structure and/orprocedures
of the Chula Vista Redevelopment Agency when each of them have economic interests in
the jurisdiction that the chula vista Redevelopment Agency operates?
CONCLUSION
Yes. The Act's conflict of interest provisions do not prohibit Councilmembers
Casteneda, McCann, Rindone, and Councilmember-elect Ramirezfromparticipating in
governmental decisions to modifii the structure and/orprocedures of the Chulu Virtu
1 On Novemb et 20,2006,the Commission received your original request, dated November 14,
2006, on behalf of Councilmembers Castaneda, McCann, and Rindone. On Novembe r 27 , 2006, the
Commission received a second letter, dated November 21,2006, in which you requested that
councilmember-elect Ramirez also be included under your original request.
2 Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear attitle2,sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code ofRegulations.
F__{s- 9


File No. A-06-219
Page No. 2
Redevelopment Agency so long as such decisions will not have anyreasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any of their economic interests.
FACTS
The City of Chula Vista's (the City) redevelopment areas are governed by the
chula vista Redevelopment Corporation ("GVRC') and the city,s redevelopment
agency ("RDA"). The CVRC is a nonprofit 501(cX3) organization. It performs design
review, planning, and preliminary approval of redeveioprient projects and was created by
the city council. The CVRC is the final decision-maker on certain actions and an
advisory board to the RDA on other actions. The CVRC has nine members: four
independent directors and five city directors (the mayor and city council). The CVRC,s
jurisdiction includes the entire territory in the existing redevelopment areas. The City has
six redevelopment areas, some of which have been merged for financial purposes. Based
upon the current geographic information systems information, there ur" uppio*imately
2,263 property owners within the redevelopment project areas and approiimately 50,4++
property owners within the City.
The city council sits as the RDA. The City is a charter city governed by a council
- consisting of four councilmembers and amayor elected from the City at-large. The city
charter and agency rules provide for the approval of resolutions and ordinancJs by the
affirmative votes of at least three members of the council unless another provision of the
charter requires otherwise. The charter provides that appropriations made after the
adoption of the budget shall require the affirmative votes of at least four members.
The council may consider modifications to the CVRC's structure and operating
procedures. Councilmembers Castaneda, McCann, and Rindone ownproperty within
500 feet of a redevelopment project area ("RPA'). Councilmember McCann Lwns three
residential rental properties and his primary residence within the City. One of his rental
properties is within 500 feet of the boundary of an RPA. Councilmembers Castaneda and
Rindone own their primary residences, which are located within 500 feet of an RpA
boundary. Councilmember-elect Rudy Ramirez also owns two properties within 500 feet
of a redevelopment area. One of the properties is his primary residence, and the other is
the property on which he operates his business. Your letter requests advice as to whether
the above councilmembers may participate in the decision to modiff the structure and/or
procedures of the CVRC.
You mentioned in our telephone conversation that one of the governmental
decisions that may take place could involve the payment of a stipendio the members of
the cvRC. Such a stipend, if paid would involve funds being paid to the
councilmembers, as they are all members of the CVRC. You also mentioned that one of
the decisions may involve changing the number of board members on the CVRC. you
have provided no other facts regarding potential decisions.


File No. A-06-219
Page No. 3
ANALYSIS
PornNrr,lr, CoNrr,rcr oF INTEREST
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifuing conflict of interest. (Section 87100, regulation lSr00@).)
The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official
makes a goveflrmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on one or more of his or her financial interests.
Steps "1, & 2: Are the council members public officials making, participating in
making or influencing a governmental decision?
As elected members of the Chula Vista City Council, Councilmembers Casteneda,
Mccann, and Rindone are public officials under the Act.3 (Section gz04g.)
Councilmember-elect Rudy Ramirez will also be a public official under the Act upon
taking office. Consequently, theymaynot make, participate in making, or otherwise use
their official positions to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on any of their economic interests.
As members of the Chula Vista City Council, they will be called upon to consider
whether the Council should approve or disapprove changes in procedure ind,/or structure of
the CVRC. Therefore, they will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using
their official positions to influence govemmental decisions.
Step 3: Do the council members have potentially disqualifying economic interests?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
3 Section 87105 provides that when a public official who holds an office specified in section
87200 has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he oishe must: (1)
immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identifu each type of economic interest involved in
the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation 1B7O2.S(b),on tle record
of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the, jiscussion
and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the
public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulations 18702.5(c) ana {UOZ.S1d) apply.
This section is applicable to the Chula Vista City Councilmembers since members of city councilJari
specified in Section 87200.


File No. A-06-219
Page No. 4
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumeiated economic
interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entitya in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a djrector, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87 1 03 (d); regulation lg7 03 .l (b));
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section 87r03(b); regulation 1g703.2);
o An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggtegating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section g7103(c);
regulation 18703.3);
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section S7103(-); regulation
18703.4);
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule
(Section 87 103; regulation I 8703.5).
Economic Interests fdentified: Appllng the above rules, the councilmembers
have the following potentially disqualifying economic interests:
Business Interest:
Councilmember-elect Ramirezhas an economic interest in his small
business entity which we reasonably assume he has an investrnent of $2,000 or
more and because, we assume, he is either the sole owner, operator, or director.
(Section 87103(c) and (d), regulation 18703.1(a) and (b).)
Councilmember McCannhas an economic interest in his business renting the
three residential rental units he owns. Presumably he has an investment of $2,000 or
more in these rental businesses, is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
manager of the rental businesses. Therefore, he has an economic interesi initre rental
businesses as business entities. (Section 87103 (a), (c), and (d).)
Real Property Interests:
a A "business entity" is defined as "any-organizationor enterprise operated forprofit, including
but not limited to a Rrog19lorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation, or
association" (Section 82005.)


File No. A-06-219
Page No. 5
councilmembers casteneda, Mccann, Rindone, and councilmember_
elect Ramirez all have economic interests in real property in which we assume
they have a $2,000 interest as follows:
C o u n ci lm e m b e r C us te n e da has a real prop erty interest in hi s residence.
Councilmember McCannhas arealproperty interest in his residence and
the three rentalproperties he owns in Chula Vista.
Councilmember Rindonehas areal property interest in his residence.
Councilmember-elect Ramirez has a real property interest in his business
property and his residence.
Sources of Income:
Councilmember'elect Ramirez has an economic interest in his business as
a source of income. Additionally, because he owns at least ten percent of the
business he has an economic interest in each of the customers olhis business from
whom he has received income or promised income that aggregates to $500 or
more within 12 months prior to the decision being made. lsection g2030.)
Councilmember McCannhas an economic interest in the income he
receives from his rental property tenants because they are sources of income to
him, as we assume his rental tenants will be paylng councilmember Mccann
income that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
being made. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.)
While you have not provided sufficient facts to determine whether the
CVRC could also be considered a source of income to the councilmembers. We
assume that the cvRC is a government agency. section g2030(b)e) provides
that salary received from a state, local, or federal agerlcy is not considered income
under the Act. Accordingly, while the cvRC may be asource of income to the
councilmembers, the CVRC would not be considered a source of income to the
councilmembers as any salary that could be athibuted to the cvRC, which is a
governmental entity, would not be considered income under the Act.
Personal Finances:
Additionally, under the "personal financial effects" rule, a conflict of interest
exists where a decision would foreseeably result in a public official's personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities (or those of his or her immediate farnily) increasing or
decreasing by $250 or more, regardless of the source of that increase or decrease.
(Section 87103; regulations 18703.5; 18704.5;18705.5(a); BeardsleyAdvice Letter, No.


File No. A-06-219
Page No. 6
I-9 9 -003 ; D ani e ls Advice Letter, No. I-98-297.)
Step 4: Are the council members' economic interests directly or indirectly involved
in the governmental decisions?
"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the
govenrmental decision. " (Section 87 I 00, regulation I S7 0a@).)
Regulation 18704.1(a) states: that"aperson, including business enttties, sources
of income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official,s
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent: (1) initiates the proceeding in
which the decision will be made by filing an apprication, ciaim, appeal,or similar
request; or (2) is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency." Undir your facts, there is nothing to
indicate that Councilmember-elect Ramirez's business or customers to his business will
be initiating proceedings or be the subject of proceedings at this time. The same applies
for Councilmember McCann's rental tenants and rental businesses. Accordingly, such
interests would be indirectly involved.s
Regulation 1870a.2@) sets forth one of the circumstances under which real
property (including rental property) in which a public official has an economic interest is
deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest,
or any part of that real property, is l0cated within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the govemmental decision.,,
The decisions you have inquired about concern changing the procedures andlor
structure of the CVRC, such as deciding the number of memters thaishould be on the
board of the CVRC. Such decisions would affect the way the CVRC is governed but
would not change the redevelopment areas themselves or affect the real [roperty.
Therefore, it appears that the redevelopment areas are not the..subject,, of the
governmental decisions you described. Thus the councilmember's real property whether
located within 500 feet of a redevelopment area or not would not be directly involved in
the governmental decisions at hand. Real property thatis not directly invoived in a
governmental decision is deemed to be indirectly involved.
5 Business entities and sources of income that arenot directly involved in governmental decisions
under the rules quoted above are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704:l16).)


File No. A-06-219
Page No. 7
Step 5: What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
govefirmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision.
Business Entities:
Regulation 18705.1(c) provides the materiality standard for indirectly involved
business entities. For a relatively small business, the standard is provided under
subsection (c)(a):
"(4) - -. the financial effect of a governmental decision on a business entity is
material if it is reasonable foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or
"(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses
for a fiscal year inthe amount of $5,000 or more; or
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.,,
You have provided no facts as to whether there will be any reasonably
foreseeable mateial financial effect on businesses owned by Councilmember-elect
Ramirez or councilmember Mccann so it will be up to thern to make this
determination based upon the above listed criteria.
Source of Income:
Regulation 18705.3 provides the materiality standard for an economic interest in
persons who are sources of income to the councilmembers. The financial effect of a
governmental decision is deemed material with respect to an individual who is a source
of income to an officialif any of the following applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000
or more; or


File No. A-06-219
Page No. 8
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under Title 2, california code of
Regulations, section I 87 0 5 .2(b). " (Regulation I g705.3 (bX3).)
You have not provided any facts that would indicate that any of the
described decisions would have areasonably foreseeable financial effect on either
Councilmember-elect Ramirez's or Councilmember McCann's sources of income.
Real
Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property that is indirectly involved in the governmentaidecision is
presumed not to be material . This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are
specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the
nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic interest, which
make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
the real property in which the official has an interest. Examples of these specific
circumstances include circumstances where the decision afflects:
(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
(c) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.
Because the decisions that you described appear to be procedural and do not
include the actual redevelopment areas as the subject of the govemmental decisions,
Councilmembers Castaneda, McCann, Rindone, and Councilmember-elect Ramirez, s real
property economic interests are deemed indirectly involved in this decision.
Personal Financial Effects :
You mentioned that some of the govemmental decisions regarding the structure
and/or procedures of the CVRC could involve deciding whether mimbers of the CVRC
should be paid a stipend. The "personal financial effects ruIe,, does not require
disqualification if the decision affects only the salary, per diem, or reimbursement for
expenses an official may receive from alocal govefirment agency, unless the decision is
to hire, fire, promote, demote, suspend without pay or otherwise take disciplinary action
against the official or a member of his or her immediate family, or to set a-salary for the


File No. A-06-219
Page No. 9
official or a member of his or her immediate family that is different from salaries paid to
other employees of the goveflrment agency in the same job classification or position.
(Section 87 700, regulation I 8705.5(b).)
Because the stipend would be paid by a local government agency and would be
the same for all of the members of the CVRC it would not disquali$z the councilmembers
from voting to create a stipend for the members of the CVRC under the personal financial
effects rule. You have provided no other facts to indicate any personal financial effect on
any of the councilmembers or councilember-elect, therefore it appears that the personal
ftnancial effects rule will not disqualify any of the councilmembers or the
councilmember-elect from participating in the decisions due to the receipt of a stipend, as
long as the above conditions are met.
Step 6: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on the Councilmember's economic interests wilI meet the applicable
materiality standard?
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the
time a govemmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. As
used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706; In
re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to
meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable . (Ibid.)
Please note that the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing
advice (In re Oglesby, supra), therefore, the foreseeability of a particular financial iffect
is a determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the
public official.
If the decisions that you describe as strictly structural and/or procedural would
make a reasonably foreseeable change to the redevelopment areas themselves there may
be a conflict. For example, if it was reasonably foreseeable that changing the number of
board members of the CVRC would make a decision to change the borders of a
redevelopment areamore likely to pass because adding a board member or two would
sway the vote balance to do so, then such a decision would lead to another decision
where the councilmembers' economic interests could be directly involved and could
make an effect on their economic interests reasonably foreseeable. In such cases, the
Commission has advised that these decisions are too interrelated to be considered
separately and an official's conflict on one decision would disqualiSz the official from the
other. (Brown Advice Letter I-01-284.) You have provided no facts to indicate that the
decisions to make changes to the structure andlor procedures of the CVRC are
interrelated to other decisions that may cause a conflict of interest, but if this situation
does arise the councilmembers are advised to seek further assistance.


File No. A-06-219
Page No. l0
Step 7: Despite a determination that one or more of the council members have a
potential conflict of interest, may the council members participate in the decisions
because the financial effects on their economic interests ar" rot distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally?
A public official who determines that his or her economic interest will experience
amatenal financial effect as a result of a governmental decision may nevertheless
participate in the decision if the financial effect on his or her interest is not
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Section 87l}3,regulation
18707 -) Because we have found no indication of a conflict of interest based upon your
facts there is no need to analyze your situation under this exception at this time.
Step 8: Despite a determination that one or more of the council members have a
potential conflict of interest may the council members participate in the decisions
because their participation is IegaIIy required?
Section 87101 (regulation 18703) provides an exception under which a public
official who has a conflict of interest in a governmental decision may nevertheless
participate. Because we have found no indication of a conflict of inierest based upon
your facts there is no need to analyze your situation under this exception at this time.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

manager of the rental businesses. Therefore, he has an economic interesi initre rental
businesses as business entities. (Section 87103 (a), (c), and (d).)
Real Property Interests:
a A "business entity" is defined as "any-organizationor enterprise operated forprofit, including
but not limited to a Rrog19lorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation, or
association" (Section 82005.)
councilmembers casteneda, Mccann, Rindone, and councilmember_
elect Ramirez all have economic interests in real property in which we assume
they have a $2,000 interest as follows:
C o u n ci lm e m b e r C us te n e da has a real prop erty interest in hi s residence.
Councilmember McCannhas arealproperty interest in his residence and
the three rentalproperties he owns in Chula Vista.
Councilmember Rindonehas areal property interest in his residence.
Councilmember-elect Ramirez has a real property interest in his business
property and his residence.
Sources of Income:
Councilmember'elect Ramirez has an economic interest in his business as
a source of income. Additionally, because he owns at least ten percent of the
business he has an economic interest in each of the customers olhis business from
whom he has received income or promised income that aggregates to $500 or
more within 12 months prior to the decision being made. lsection g2030.)
Councilmember McCannhas an economic interest in the income he
receives from his rental property tenants because they are sources of income to
him, as we assume his rental tenants will be paylng councilmember Mccann
income that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
being made. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.)
While you have not provided sufficient facts to determine whether the
CVRC could also be considered a source of income to the councilmembers. We
assume that the cvRC is a government agency. section g2030(b)e) provides
that salary received from a state, local, or federal agerlcy is not considered income
under the Act. Accordingly, while the cvRC may be asource of income to the
councilmembers, the CVRC would not be considered a source of income to the
councilmembers as any salary that could be athibuted to the cvRC, which is a
governmental entity, would not be considered income under the Act.
Personal Finances:
Additionally, under the "personal financial effects" rule, a conflict of interest
exists where a decision would foreseeably result in a public official's personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities (or those of his or her immediate farnily) increasing or
decreasing by $250 or more, regardless of the source of that increase or decrease.
(Section 87103; regulations 18703.5; 18704.5;18705.5(a); BeardsleyAdvice Letter, No.
I-9 9 -003 ; D ani e ls Advice Letter, No. I-98-297.)
Step 4: Are the council members' economic interests directly or indirectly involved
in the governmental decisions?
"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the
govenrmental decision. " (Section 87 I 00, regulation I S7 0a@).)
Regulation 18704.1(a) states: that"aperson, including business enttties, sources
of income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official,s
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent: (1) initiates the proceeding in
which the decision will be made by filing an apprication, ciaim, appeal,or similar
request; or (2) is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency." Undir your facts, there is nothing to
indicate that Councilmember-elect Ramirez's business or customers to his business will
be initiating proceedings or be the subject of proceedings at this time. The same applies
for Councilmember McCann's rental tenants and rental businesses. Accordingly, such
interests would be indirectly involved.s
Regulation 1870a.2@) sets forth one of the circumstances under which real
property (including rental property) in which a public official has an economic interest is
deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest,
or any part of that real property, is l0cated within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the govemmental decision.,,
The decisions you have inquired about concern changing the procedures andlor
structure of the CVRC, such as deciding the number of memters thaishould be on the
board of the CVRC. Such decisions would affect the way the CVRC is governed but
would not change the redevelopment areas themselves or affect the real [roperty.
Therefore, it appears that the redevelopment areas are not the..subject,, of the
governmental decisions you described. Thus the councilmember's real property whether
located within 500 feet of a redevelopment area or not would not be directly involved in
the governmental decisions at hand. Real property thatis not directly invoived in a
governmental decision is deemed to be indirectly involved.
5 Business entities and sources of income that arenot directly involved in governmental decisions
under the rules quoted above are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704:l16).)
Step 5: What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
govefirmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision.
Business Entities:
Regulation 18705.1(c) provides the materiality standard for indirectly involved
business entities. For a relatively small business, the standard is provided under
subsection (c)(a):
"(4) - -. the financial effect of a governmental decision on a business entity is
material if it is reasonable foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or
"(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses
for a fiscal year inthe amount of $5,000 or more; or
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.,,
You have provided no facts as to whether there will be any reasonably
foreseeable mateial financial effect on businesses owned by Councilmember-elect
Ramirez or councilmember Mccann so it will be up to thern to make this
determination based upon the above listed criteria.
Source of Income:
Regulation 18705.3 provides the materiality standard for an economic interest in
persons who are sources of income to the councilmembers. The financial effect of a
governmental decision is deemed material with respect to an individual who is a source
of income to an officialif any of the following applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000
or more; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under Title 2, california code of
Regulations, section I 87 0 5 .2(b). " (Regulation I g705.3 (bX3).)
You have not provided any facts that would indicate that any of the
described decisions would have areasonably foreseeable financial effect on either
Councilmember-elect Ramirez's or Councilmember McCann's sources of income.
Real
Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property that is indirectly involved in the governmentaidecision is
presumed not to be material . This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are
specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the
nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic interest, which
make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
the real property in which the official has an interest. Examples of these specific
circumstances include circumstances where the decision afflects:
(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
(c) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.
Because the decisions that you described appear to be procedural and do not
include the actual redevelopment areas as the subject of the govemmental decisions,
Councilmembers Castaneda, McCann, Rindone, and Councilmember-elect Ramirez, s real
property economic interests are deemed indirectly involved in this decision.
Personal Financial Effects :
You mentioned that some of the govemmental decisions regarding the structure
and/or procedures of the CVRC could involve deciding whether mimbers of the CVRC
should be paid a stipend. The "personal financial effects ruIe,, does not require
disqualification if the decision affects only the salary, per diem, or reimbursement for
expenses an official may receive from alocal govefirment agency, unless the decision is
to hire, fire, promote, demote, suspend without pay or otherwise take disciplinary action
against the official or a member of his or her immediate family, or to set a-salary for the
official or a member of his or her immediate family that is different from salaries paid to
other employees of the goveflrment agency in the same job classification or position.
(Section 87 700, regulation I 8705.5(b).)
Because the stipend would be paid by a local government agency and would be
the same for all of the members of the CVRC it would not disquali$z the councilmembers
from voting to create a stipend for the members of the CVRC under the personal financial
effects rule. You have provided no other facts to indicate any personal financial effect on
any of the councilmembers or councilember-elect, therefore it appears that the personal
ftnancial effects rule will not disqualify any of the councilmembers or the
councilmember-elect from participating in the decisions due to the receipt of a stipend, as
long as the above conditions are met.
Step 6: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on the Councilmember's economic interests wilI meet the applicable
materiality standard?
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the
time a govemmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. As
used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706; In
re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to
meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable . (Ibid.)
Please note that the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing
advice (In re Oglesby, supra), therefore, the foreseeability of a particular financial iffect
is a determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the
public official.
If the decisions that you describe as strictly structural and/or procedural would
make a reasonably foreseeable change to the redevelopment areas themselves there may
be a conflict. For example, if it was reasonably foreseeable that changing the number of
board members of the CVRC would make a decision to change the borders of a
redevelopment areamore likely to pass because adding a board member or two would
sway the vote balance to do so, then such a decision would lead to another decision
where the councilmembers' economic interests could be directly involved and could
make an effect on their economic interests reasonably foreseeable. In such cases, the
Commission has advised that these decisions are too interrelated to be considered
separately and an official's conflict on one decision would disqualiSz the official from the
other. (Brown Advice Letter I-01-284.) You have provided no facts to indicate that the
decisions to make changes to the structure andlor procedures of the CVRC are
interrelated to other decisions that may cause a conflict of interest, but if this situation
does arise the councilmembers are advised to seek further assistance.


Page No. l0
Step 7: Despite a determination that one or more of the council members have a
potential conflict of interest, may the council members participate in the decisions
because the financial effects on their economic interests ar" rot distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally?
A public official who determines that his or her economic interest will experience
amatenal financial effect as a result of a governmental decision may nevertheless
participate in the decision if the financial effect on his or her interest is not
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Section 87l}3,regulation
18707 -) Because we have found no indication of a conflict of interest based upon your
facts there is no need to analyze your situation under this exception at this time.
Step 8: Despite a determination that one or more of the council members have a
potential conflict of interest may the council members participate in the decisions
because their participation is IegaIIy required?
Section 87101 (regulation 18703) provides an exception under which a public
official who has a conflict of interest in a governmental decision may nevertheless
participate. Because we have found no indication of a conflict of inierest based upon
your facts there is no need to analyze your situation under this exception at this time.

FACTS The City of Chula Vista's (the City) redevelopment areas are governed by the chula vista Redevelopment Corporation ("GVRC') and the city,s redevelopment agency ("RDA"). The CVRC is a nonprofit 501(cX3) organization. It performs design review, planning, and preliminary approval of redeveioprient projects and was created by the city council. The CVRC is the final decision-maker on certain actions and an advisory board to the RDA on other actions. The CVRC has nine members: four independent directors and five city directors (the mayor and city council). The CVRC,s jurisdiction includes the entire territory in the existing redevelopment areas. The City has six redevelopment areas, some of which have been merged for financial purposes. Based upon the current geographic information systems information, there ur" uppio*imately 2,263 property owners within the redevelopment project areas and approiimately 50,4++ property owners within the City. The city council sits as the RDA. The City is a charter city governed by a council - consisting of four councilmembers and amayor elected from the City at-large. The city charter and agency rules provide for the approval of resolutions and ordinancJs by the affirmative votes of at least three members of the council unless another provision of the charter requires otherwise. The charter provides that appropriations made after the adoption of the budget shall require the affirmative votes of at least four members. The council may consider modifications to the CVRC's structure and operating procedures. Councilmembers Castaneda, McCann, and Rindone ownproperty within 500 feet
